policy_statement: An Environmental Energy Plan will be drawn up by end of 2002, based on energy balances and favoring renewable sources and technological innovation.
mechanism_description: It draws up an environmental energy plan that favors renewable sources and technological innovation, supporting a shift away from fossil-based energy and lowering power-sector emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Energy planning + renewable deployment → ↓ fossil electricity generation → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public-sector energy planning, targets, and enabling actions that prioritize renewable energy and innovation in the electricity supply mix.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Increased renewable generation (and often associated grid upgrades/flexibility) substitutes for coal/oil/gas combustion in power plants, lowering combustion-related CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions per kWh.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in jurisdiction-wide and grid-average electricity emissions intensity and total power-sector GHG emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing electricity-sector GHG emissions by shifting generation from fossil fuels to renewables.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Planning that enables renewables → displacement of fossil generation → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such plans can also improve energy security, affordability, or resilience, the main climate-relevant outcome common to all instances of this mechanism is emissions mitigation through grid decarbonization.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a plan labeled “environmental energy plan” focuses mainly on efficiency (reducing demand) it could align with Resource Efficiency; if it focuses mainly on resilience/hardening it could align with Adaptation. However, the canonical mechanism explicitly specifies renewables-led grid decarbonization, so Mitigation dominates.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is energy planning that favors renewables to decarbonize the grid has its largest climate effect via reduced power-sector emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., utility-scale renewables vs distributed solar, storage, grid modernization) require policy-level details and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “plan” is purely advisory with no implementation or is primarily about adaptation/resilience of the energy system rather than changing the generation mix, the real-world dominant effect could shift; under the stated mechanism (renewables → grid decarbonization) the primary category remains Mitigation.
key_indicators: “Environmental Energy Plan”; “shall be drawn up”; “by the end of the year 2002”; “based on the individual energy balances”; “favouring: renewable sources and technological innovation”
co_benefits: Energy security; air quality improvement; innovation/industrial development
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit quantitative targets or implementation tools (e.g., mandates, incentives, procurement) are specified—only the creation of a plan—so classification stays at planning/enabling rather than target-setting or regulatory action.
